speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm recording.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is January 30, 1995. We're interviewing Sarah Salamon, S-A-R-A-H S-A-L-A-M-O-N. Sarah, in Poland, was known as Sonya, S-O-N-Y-A, Bimko, B-I-M-K-O. While she was in Auschwitz, she went under the name of Sasha, S-A-S-H-A.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sasha.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. Just tell us where we are again.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're now in Toronto, Canada, and the language we-- we're using is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, and camera is set.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sarah, will you tell us where you were born and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lublin. Street is [NON-ENGLISH], the first of May. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know how old I--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And you-- your parents lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived in-- in what? In a house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a house

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a house owned by your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father built it for us when I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, this is when you got married. What about-- what did you grow-- when you were growing up with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, then I lived with my parents in the building. We got a eight-- a big apartment on [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: The street is [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and you had one flat on-- of the eight flats?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mine parents, not me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I married, we-- I moved-- moved out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you live-- where you lived, was it mainly Jewish? Or did you have non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you went to school, what school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To a not-- a private school. What school? High school?

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll start with [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is normal. And then I went--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you say normal--

speaker: interviewee
text: University.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, before. When you went to school in elementary school, did you go to a Jewish school? Or did you go to a public school? A polish school? I got-- I went to a Jewish school for a short time in the [NON-ENGLISH]. And how did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Close. It was close to mine mother's--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you walked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Or sometimes, the daddy give me a lift.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had a car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father-- what was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a medical doctor

speaker: interviewer
text: In?

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked in a hospital. And he-- he went to sick people after the time. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a religious family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father, not. But the mother, yes. The mother comes from a very religious family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were telling me a story about your mother disciplining you about what you were wearing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't like it, what I wanted to wear it, like all mothers. But I help myself. My father was watching me. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Elementary school, you went to Bais Yaakov.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, I went a normal school.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say normal school. What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A regular high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gymnasium-- Federalski Gymnasium, under the name [NON-ENGLISH]. There was a factory from beer, and the high school. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and-- and did you enjoy school? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I liked very much, school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- was it-- was it Polish teachers that were teaching you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish, German, even a little bit English, sometimes. But this is private teachers was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages where you able to speak then? Did you learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: German. Polish, I didn't have to learn. A little bit Hebrew and a little English, because I-- when I came here, I could understand. That helped me a lot. And that-- that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after school, did you participate in any activities, any Jewish activities, Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes, yes, but not much. I was always with friends from the same class.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of things would you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean, what I would do?

speaker: interviewer
text: What I-- well, did you participate in Zionist organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during the summer, when you weren't going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, I went with my-- with my father to Paris. Every doctor, he-- he went once a year to Paris from-- I don't know if you will understand this-- this-- from this poviat. This is like-- like this part of--

speaker: interviewer
text: The area? The community?

speaker: interviewee
text: The area. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we went four weeks every month, because my mother didn't want to go with my father. She will not go with a airplane Shabbos.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they got a interview, all the medical people from around the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a medical conference?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you went with your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: With my father, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to-- you went by airplane then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: As you grew up, you-- you went through high school, and then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, I went to university.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Lublin?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were there--

speaker: interviewee
text: In medical school, two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened? Then the war came out. And my father came and picked me up. So [INAUDIBLE]-- don't take nothing. Come home. They knew what was going on. So you-- you did two years, and then you came back to Lublin? And-- and what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came back home.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, before. You know, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: You did two years in Lublin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Vienna. And then you came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then they arrested me.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in between--

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't long.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] It was quick. They make-- I don't know to-- in English, this pronounce. [SIGHS] They take all Jewish people, and included me, to a jail in Lublin. We-- I don't know how many months I was there, and then to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you know what year this was?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Before that, were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, can you tell us, was it a match? Were you matched with your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean? No, not a match.

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't a match?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked with my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a-- a medical student with my husband-- with my father, I mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stanley Litwinski.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so, what do you-- do you remember what year you were married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh-- [SIGHS] not-- I-- I don't remember this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before-- sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you went to Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I went to-- to Vienna, I got a baby already.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My-- my mother was the babysitter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. Brenda? [INAUDIBLE] your-- your right hand?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. Thank you. You-- so you-- you were married before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had how many children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two.

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry and Barbara. But Barbara was nine months old when-- [SIGHS] yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- when you were married--

speaker: interviewee
text: When I married?

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you-- did you stay with your parents? Did you live with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but the-- the child was there. My mother didn't give me the child. She didn't trust me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she took-- she helped you take care of the-- the child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The child is hers, she said. And he called her mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Henry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you said something about your father building a house for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about that? For you and your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was three-- I think, three bedrooms, a dining room, living room, and a kitchen. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: One story? Two stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was one story.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a separate house that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: A separate house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did it have running water in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And plumbing?

speaker: interviewee
text: And plumbing and everything. It's my father. So too, with everything, should be ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- you had the children before the war and before you went to Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: One, I got before, and one later.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came back from Vienna--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said something about nursing school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. This, I got between high school. I took nursing school. I graduated nursing school.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Lublin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from nursing school, then you went to Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Then I got a lot easier-- a lots thing they teach about nursing there, in the beginning. And then, it was very hard. I worked day and night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us something about your-- your parents, what life was like with your parents when you were living in Lublin before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, it was wonderful. I don't think, if you find parents like this was-- I was the only child. My mother couldn't have more children, so I was their whole life for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Sabbath like?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in-- my mother, around the house, like a-- a religious person do it, because she was from a very religious family, rabbis. Mostly, they graduated the-- the Jewish University, the cousins. The family was very religious. And so my mother tried to keep the-- her religious very strictly.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your father brought you back from Vienna, what-- what happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you come back home and stay at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, I got in my-- I was in my home. But it was close, so the child was mostly with the mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which-- both children? Henry and Barbara?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Barbara-- Barbara got nine months, when I put her in the church, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll come-- we'll come to that. What-- what happened with Henry when--

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry? A friend my mother's, a Christian woman, she got a-- a son in Henry's age. And this child died, hers. And she got the-- how should I say? The papers, this [NON-ENGLISH], they call.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a birth certificate?

speaker: interviewee
text: A birth certificate. And she give it. All right. This cost-- used to cost a lot of money. And she give it to my mother. Don't cry, Esther, she says, I have mine child's birth certificate, and I will take the child and I will give care of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the child was with the-- my mother's friend, an older woman, a very nice person. And she lives on the-- on the same street with my mother. And-- Do you remember what the-- the-- the street name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the boy was with-- he called this lady grandmother, bapchu. And once, shortly before the German-- the Germans went-- the Russians came into Lublin, this child was-- she let him more be freely in-- in the street to play with kids. And then, went a Polish policeman-- his name is Dulski. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] He was-- yeah, he was a policeman, but he never weared police clothes. Just with the SS men. They went through. And mine son played-- this is what the lady told me later, because I ran away after the war was finished. And I went to Poland. And I says, this child must be alive. Like.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, when I came-- and the Polish policeman says to the SS man, this is Jude-- of the-- of the-- the boy. He knew, because he lived not far away from [? us. ?] The-- that German took the revolver and shot him. And that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember-- do you know when this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. 10 days before the Russians came in to Lublin. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then, when the war was end, because I was a witness in Leninburg, was a protest from the SS man. I was-- three weeks, I was by a microphone and explain what was it, how I was in-- I-- I-- they took me to the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll talk about that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a little bit, we'll come back to that. Do you remember the lady's name who took care of Henry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she's not alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. But can you-- can you give us her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Katalina Litkinska.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's-- and-- and you said you-- you gave Henry-- when was this that she started to take care of Henry?

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they took me to jail. And my mother was already in-- they took her before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother was also in jail at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the jail. They take her to Treblinka, to another concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what about Barbara?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Barbara, mine father arranged with a priest-- I didn't know even this-- that he should give care of the baby. Then they arranged-- when they-- when they took us, and I got the baby on mine arm, I should leave on the steps and run back. This is-- I don't know that they didn't-- they was blind, the SS, or something, that they don't see me running.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had-- so you had Barbara in the jail with you, while--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left her on the step of the-- in the church.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my father arrangement with the priest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know this priest beforehand?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. He used to come every Friday for supper to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] he's a priest. That's all. A priest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and the church was where?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the street, [? Zamoiska. ?] But how the priest called-- the-- the church, he called, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were taken to jail.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's when you put Barbara on the church steps.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, how long were you in jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: In jail, I was about six, seven months, until they maked a transport to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in jail, you-- who-- who was responsible for you? Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody. The SS, if they wanted, they came, took every day another person. And you just hear a shot outside. That's all. You wasn't a human being, at this time. You-- you [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. They call us Jewish whore, Jewish this-- you know? Very, very--

speaker: interviewer
text: And these were Germans who were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --guarding you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after six months, then--

speaker: interviewee
text: They took me, transport. They make a big transport into Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by normal?

speaker: interviewee
text: They tell-- tell us to go out from the jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were, at the time, a couple hundred in big trucks. And they took us to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: By truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: By truck. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and when you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, they [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], right and left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what that meant?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but I got the idea what this meant. And [SIGHS] we was on the [NON-ENGLISH], number 25.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's where you went, you were sent to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I don't know how we survived the [NON-ENGLISH]. Then, they give us-- you know, I was young-- I don't know, 18 or 19 year old. That's all. And they took-- give me off a block the change. There, Yoli. I met Yoli and Ilka. Everybody thought that we are sisters. And they take me in. I didn't sleep now in a cabin together with the fluechtlinge.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fluechtlinge, this is, in German, the people what they are under arrest. And she says-- she came and took us, a couple girls what we was the special one. I don't know. Because we was young. And then, she took us in her block. And to me, she says, you come with me. And she took me in in a little room what's very nice, clean, nice bed, the nice bedding. And we was, like everybody know in Auschwitz, this is the three sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did--

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and then, she told me, once, they make our registration, and she tell me, Sasha, you say that you are a mischling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain what that is?

speaker: interviewee
text: A mischling? The father is Catholic, and the mother was Jewish. And I give it this. And thanks to this, what she teach me, I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what do you mean, you survived? What you mean that-- that-- that because of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did-- I-- Because of that, yeah. And they took we once in crematorium. I was already a couple minute-- a couple second, I saw the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], the water that came, [NON-ENGLISH]. And I started to feel already scratching in the throat. And there was 3,000 women this time. And I saw so many. And everybody naked. And one day came in a SS man and hold a blanket, and grabbed me, and took me out, put a-- a blanket-- the-- the gray blanket, and took me out, and then, took me in a car back to the-- to the block where I was. And one French girl was between, a young girl, maybe 16, 15 year old. They took her out, too, This way, we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you think that's because you had-- your-- you said that your father was Catholic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that I'm a mischling. That I-- I'm-- this is not Jewish. It's not clear Jewish blood. And then, they started to call me on the [NON-ENGLISH]-- this is to the high SS man-- what I saw it when I was in-- in the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you say-- tell us what a [NON-ENGLISH] is?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-- a shower, a big room that they pack in a couple thousand people, and they let the water running.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it water that came out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Zyklon came out, the gas. And there was, on the floor, you know, so much naked bodies. And the SS man came in, and put the blanket over me, and grabbed me, and took me back to-- to-- to the block.

speaker: interviewer
text: Block 25. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Not block-- that of Yoli was the block [NON-ENGLISH]. And she give care a while. And then, I started to go, and they asked me questions. And they tell me right away, you-- where you was? Do you remember? I said sure. I was in the [NON-ENGLISH]. [BACKGROUND NOISE] But I didn't finish mine bath, maybe a second or two. And Mr. Hessler come and take me out, because I wasn't allowed-- they call me out. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And this, I went maybe-- maybe three, four weeks, every day. And they asked me the same thing. They give me to eat. They give me nice clothes, warm. This-- this were in the winter time. And then, they let me go. Very nice behaved, the SS. But not long. Then they started to show really who this are. Then they started to hit me. So 18 times, I was in the-- in-- in the [NON-ENGLISH]. And they asked me always the same thing-- you was in crematorium. They told me this, right away. This is not a [NON-ENGLISH]. But I knew.
